---
title: "Fundamentals of Missing Data"
subtitle: "Faculty Development Program for Universidad César Vallejo, Peru"
author: 
  - name: Steven J. Pierce
    orcid: 0000-0002-0679-3019
    email: pierces1@msu.edu
institute: "[Center for Statistical Training and Consulting](https://cstat.msu.edu)"
date: 2026-07-20
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-notes: "1.5 hr (70 min talk + 20 min Q & A) My talk today focuses on 
  some concepts and issues related to missing data in scientific research 
  contexts. My expertise lies in applied statistics, so I'll focus on aspects of 
  missing data relevant to quantitative methods and statistical analyses. Even 
  still, missing data is a big topic and this is just a short talk, so my goal 
  is to teach some fundamental ideas, share some insights and practical advice, 
  then offer resources you can use to learn more on your own. Jot down either my 
  email address or the link you see at the bottom of the screen. My slides, 
  complete with speaker notes, reference list, and links to resources, will be 
  available from that link. 
  <br><br>
  [*Display at 1920 x 1080 or 1280 x 720 resolution*. In speaker notes, the 
  symbol ▶ marks  when to press the spacebar or click the right arrow to reveal 
  the next animated element on a slide.]"
csl: apa-numeric-superscript-brackets.csl
bibliography: references.bib
format: 
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default, CSTAT_theme.scss]
    controls: true
    slide-number: c/t
    show-notes: false
    header-logo: graphics/Combomark-Horiz_Green-RGB.png
    footer: <a href="https://github.com/sjpierce/FMD2026">https://github.com/sjpierce/FMD2026</a>
    logo: graphics/CSTAT_5x5_Transparent.png
    link-external-icon: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
filters:
  - reveal-header
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra) # Functions for formatting nice tables. 
library(rgl)        # for cube3d()
library(mice)       # for the boys dataset
library(ggmice)     # for plot_pattern
library(piercer)    # for cvv_missingnes(), var_missingness()
```

## Outline
* What is missing data? 
* Why do we end up with missing data? 
* Why should we care about missing data? 
* How should we start addressing missing data issues? 
* How can we diagnose the missing data issues for a given study? 
* How should we solve missing data issues?

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
I've organized this talk around answering a few simple questions. They are: 

* What is missing data? 
* Why do we end up with missing data? 
* Why should we care about missing data? 
* How should we start addressing missing data issues? 
* How can we diagnose the missing data issues for a given study? 
* How should we solve missing data issues?

:::
::::

# What is missing data? {background-image="graphics/Woman_Raising_Hand.jpg" fig-alt="Photo of a woman raising her hand to ask a question."  background-size="contain" background-position="right" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="45%"}
Missing data (MD) are measurements you want or intended to collect but did not 
get [@Fernández-García-RN4151]

::: {.incremental}
* Having MD is *common* in research studies
* If you do much research, you *will* run into MD
:::

::::
:::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 1-2 min by here.*

In the simplest terms, missing data are simply measurements that you want or
intended to collect but did not get. While you can sometimes categorize what
kind of missing data you have or identify why they are missing, the heart of the
problem is that not having all the desired data means you have to decide how to
adapt your plans for completing the study.

▶ Having missing data is a common issue afflicting research and evaluation 
studies. Entirely avoiding it is difficult and rare.

▶ If you do much research work, you *will* eventually run into an issue with 
missing data. Therefore, it's worth learning about the issues surrounding 
missing data and the consequences of how we deal with them.
:::
::::

# Why do we end up with missing data? 
Data collection doesn't always go according to plan...
<br> <br> 

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-factors
#| tbl-cap: Factors That May Lead to Missing Data

data.frame(Human = c("Participant behavior", "Researcher errors", 
                     "Partner behavior"),
           Other = c("Equipment failures", "Records/Databases", 
                     "Unusual events")) |>   
  kable(format = "html", 
        col.names = c("Human Factors", "Other Factors")) |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, lightable_options = c("hover"), 
                html_font = "Lato")
```
:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 2-3 min by here.*

Why do we end up with missing data? Like many things in life, data collection
does not always go according to plan. ▶ 

Table 1 here shows a few examples of types of things that can go wrong. Human
factors often contribute to the occurrence of missing data. For example, a
survey participant may skip a question without recording any response, offer an
illegible or irrelevant answer, refuse to answer it, or simply say "I don't
know". They may skip whole instruments or entire data collection visits. But
participants are only one human cause of missing data. Researchers occasionally
make errors that lead to missing data. Imagine forgetting to take a data
collection form with you, or programming the skip pattern for an online survey
incorrectly. Sometimes, our research partners' decisions or behavior can prevent
obtaining some data we wanted. Perhaps they object to sharing something with
you, or were not diligent about logging some events of interest.

Other things can go wrong too. Perhaps a physical instrument breaks, or its
battery dies, or your online survey app crashes. If you're using an
organization's records or databases, those may be incomplete, difficult to
search for relevant data, coded in unhelpful ways, or could have been lost or
damaged or even destroyed according to some retention policy. In terms of
events, data collection for one of my projects was disrupted by an unusually 
strong hurricane in Florida.
:::
::::

# Missing Data & Project Lifecycle

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 9
//| fig-height: 6

digraph WhenMD {
rankdir="TB";

node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Plan [label = "Study Planning & Design"]
Collect [label = "Data Collection"]
Enter [label = "Data Entry"]
Manage [label = "Data Storage & Management"]
Analyze [label = "Data Analysis"]
Plan -> Collect -> Enter -> Manage -> Analyze
}
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 4 min by here.*

Missing data result from events at various points in a project's lifecycle and 
that may affect your options to handle the problem. At the planning stage, one 
could leave out an item by accident while designing an instrument. 

Most missing data is probably a result of things happening during data 
collection events. If you are collecting data from students in a school setting,
some students in your sample might be absent due to illness on the day you
visit. That could leave you with missing data for them.  

However, there are post-data collection events that can cause missing data too.
Paper files might get lost or damaged after data collection before anyone enters
them into an electronic data file. Another possibility is that your staff may
accidentally skip one or more items that were actually recorded when entering 
data from paper forms into electronic files.

Similarly, at the data storage and management stage before analyses begin,
someone might make a mistake while working with a data file or a computer hard
drive might fail. Finally, sometimes we make errors when programming data 
analyses that cause missing data, such as inaccurately recoding a variable. 

Vigilance is warranted throughout the project lifecycle.
:::
::::

# Why should we care about missing data?  {style="padding: 0.25em .5em; background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, .5); backdrop-filter: blur(5px); box-shadow: 0 0 1rem 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .5); border-radius: 20px; text-align: left;"  background-image="graphics/Hands_Raised.jpg" fig-alt="Photo of 10 hands raised, as if many people have questions."  background-size="contain" background-position="bottom" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 5-6 min by here.*

So, I've defined missing data and identified some of the ways we can end up with
it. But why should we care about it? That's the crucial question I'll answer 
next. 
:::
::::

## Ethical Guidelines {.smaller}
Handling missing data well is an ethics issue. An ethical statistical 
practitioner: [@ASACPE-RN8343]

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="25%"}
![](graphics/ASA_logo_no_title_tagRGB.png){fig-alt="American Statistical Association logo." width=150% height=150%}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

::: {.incremental}
* "Uses methodology and data that are valid, relevant, and appropriate, without 
  favoritism or prejudice, and in a manner intended to produce valid, 
  interpretable, and reproducible results."
* "Communicates data sources and fitness for use, including data generation and 
  collection processes and known biases." 
* "Communicates data processing and transformation procedures, including missing 
   data handling."

:::

:::

::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 5-6 min by here.*

Perhaps the broadest answer to why we should care about missing data is that 
this is an ethics issue. Here I'm going to draw on the ethical guidelines for 
a professional organization to which I belong. The American Statistical 
Association's guidelines include a section on professional integrity that says 
an ethical statistical practitioner: 

▶ "Uses methodology and data that are valid, relevant, and appropriate, without 
favoritism or prejudice, and in a manner intended to produce valid, 
interpretable, and reproducible results." 

▶ There is also a section on integrity of data and methods that says an ethical 
practitioner "Communicates data sources and fitness for use, including data
generation and collection processes and known biases." and

▶ "Communicates data processing and transformation procedures, including 
missing data handling."

Other professional societies also have ethics codes that would also touch on 
these ideas about integrity. 
:::
::::

## Research Ethics
The ethical quality of an analysis depends on the credibility of its 
assumptions [@Enders-RN8974] 

::: {.incremental}
* Analyses with missing data must make assumptions, some of which 
  cannot be empirically tested
* Rigor demands using methods that can plausibly yield valid, unbiased 
  estimates
* Poor assumptions lead to bias & misleading conclusions
* Transparency requires reporting extent of missing data, assumptions, 
  rationales, & how we adapted the analyses
  
:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 7 min by here.*

The relevance of those ASA guidelines to the handling of missing data becomes 
clearer when we consider a paper by Craig Enders and Amanda Gottschall. They 
articulated the relationship between how we handle missing data and the ethical 
quality of a study. They start from the premise that the ethical quality of an 
analysis depends on the credibility of its assumptions.

▶ They go on to point out that analyzing data with missing values requires 
making assumptions, some of which cannot be empirically tested. Which 
assumptions are reasonable and defensible will vary across data sets and 
studies. 

▶ Ultimately, scientific rigor and ethics demand using methods that can 
plausibly yield unbiased estimates of the quantities we want to study.

▶ Conducting analyses built on poor assumptions about missing data leads to 
biased, invalid, and misleading results. It is unethical to rely on them. 

▶ It follows then that transparency requires reporting things like the extent 
of missing data, our assumptions about them, supporting rationales, and how we 
adapted our analyses to deal with the missing data. 
:::
::::


## Scientific Activities 
There are 3 major scientific activities that can be affected by missing data 
[@McKnight-RN1296]

::: {.incremental}
* Making structured observations of constructs
* Using observations to draw inferences about relationships between constructs
* Generalizing results to populations beyond the collected sample
::: 

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 8-9 min by here.*

Broadly speaking, scientific work consists of three major activities, each of
which has associated methodology concerns. 

* ▶  First, we put effort into making structured observations of constructs. 
We have to be concerned about how missing data affects measurement of the 
constructs we want to study. 
* ▶  Second, we use those observations to draw inferences about relationships 
between constructs. Here, we have to consider how missing data could affect the 
internal validity of our inferences. 
* ▶  Finally, we often want to generalize our results to a broader population 
than the sample we collected. Missing data can affect the generalizability of 
our results. 

Let's explore each of those ideas in more detail.
:::
::::

## Measurement Consequences [@McKnight-RN1296]

:::{.incremental}
* Availability of constructs 
* Decreased reliability due to increased error variance
* Bias from poor content coverage
* Construct validity

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 9-10 min by here.*

* ▶  Missing data can affect the availability of a construct for your analyses. 
  The extreme case would be a construct measured by a single variable.
  If that's missing, then the entire construct is missing for that individual 
  and it may be very difficult to recover that information. Missing data is still 
  a problem when we use multiple indicators to measure a construct, though 
  perhaps less severe, and it may depend on how you combine items to create the 
  score. 
* ▶  Missing data decreases the reliability and construct validity of our 
  measures. Internal consistency reliability decreases because missing data 
  increases the error variance in a measure. You may recall that reliability is 
  a function of how many items are used, so fewer observed items leads to lower 
  reliability. 
* ▶  Further, some items may be more important indicators of a construct than 
  others. You may get a biased estimate of the construct due to poorer content 
  coverage if they are missing.
* ▶  Construct validity depends on both reliability and content coverage, so 
  missing data is a threat to construct validity.
  
:::
::::

## Internal Validity Consequences [@Fernández-García-RN4151; @McKnight-RN1296]

:::{.incremental}
* Selection bias
* Compromised randomization
* Power and precision 
* Inaccurate model assumptions

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.3rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 11 min by here.*

* ▶  Missing data is a threat to a study's internal validity for several 
  reasons. Selection bias can result from systematic differences between 
  subgroups of participants on constructs relevant to the study conclusions. For 
  example, those subgroups could be treatment versus control groups, people who 
  complete the study versus drop out, or those who have complete data versus 
  incomplete data. These differences may manifest with respect to demographic 
  variables, covariates, predictors, mediators, moderators, or outcomes. Imagine 
  evaluating a longitudinal health study where people who start out with worst 
  symptoms drop out before the posttest more often than those with mild 
  symptoms. Dropping out leads to missing data at posttest. If we fail to handle 
  that properly in the analysis, we may draw inaccurate conclusions about the 
  longitudinal trend in outcome scores. Or perhaps the retention rates differ 
  between treatment and control groups in that same study. Selection biases from 
  such differences reduce the sample representativeness and become rival 
  explanations for findings, making them a threat to internal validity.
* ▶  We use randomization to make groups equivalent on measured and unmeasured 
  confounding variables as a way to strengthen validity. That works best in 
  large samples. However, missing data can compromise that effect of 
  randomization by reducing sample size. It can also reduce randomization's 
  ability to achieve equivalence between groups on confounding variables when 
  data are missing differentially across the groups. That reduces internal 
  validity. 
* ▶  Most statistical software defaults to listwise deletion of cases that have 
  any missing values on the variables involved in an analysis. Reducing the 
  sample size will always reduce both power to detect effects and the precision 
  of effect size estimates. In addition to reducing overall sample size, missing 
  data may lead to more unbalanced sample sizes between groups in a study, which 
  also reduces statistical power in many models. 
* ▶  Finally, missing data can change the distributions of variables in ways 
  that invalidate the assumptions of various statistical models. Some models are 
  more robust than others, but making inaccurate modeling assumptions risks 
  running analyses that will yield misleading conclusions. That's another threat 
  to internal validity. 
  
:::
::::

## Generalizability Consequences [@Fernández-García-RN4151; @McKnight-RN1296]

A representative sample is crucial to generalizing to the intended population!

* Theory development & cumulative knowledge
* Policy & decision-making

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 14 min by here.*

We draw samples to study populations when it isn't feasible to collect the data
we want from the entire population. The key to getting inferences that properly
generalize from a sample to the intended population is getting a representative
sample. Biases creep into findings when we use non-representative samples.
Missing data can introduce systematic biases into our samples. Suppose you're
studying a behavior that's illegal or stigmatized in some way. Some people who
engage in it may be reluctant to provide all the data you want. If we ignore the
missing data problem, listwise deletion will result in analyzing the subset of
the intended population that's willing to answer our questions. That in effect
changes the population to which we could legitimately generalize our findings
from "people who engage in behavior x" to "people who engage in behavior x and
provided complete data". Many people fail to attend to that though and assume
their results still apply to the original intended population.

That has consequences for developing and testing scientific theories and 
accumulating knowledge across studies. An example is the effect of publication 
bias, also called the file drawer problem, on meta-analyses. We know that 
studies with non-significant findings are less likely to get published. That 
means the published literature has a missing data problem that can seriously 
distort the results of meta-analyses that aim to summarize results of a 
collection of published papers. 

Finally, the big picture here is that when missing data impair generalizability, 
we may be using inaccurate or misleading findings to inform public policy and 
important decisions.
:::
::::

# Audience Questions (1) 
Do you have questions about what missing data is and why it is important? 

::: {.notes}
*Timing: ~ 16 min by here. Continue by 21 min.*

Let's pause to see if there are any questions about the material I've covered 
so far. 
:::

# How should we start addressing missing data issues? {style="padding: 0.25em .5em; background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, .5); backdrop-filter: blur(5px); box-shadow: 0 0 1rem 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .5); border-radius: 20px; text-align: left;" background-image="graphics/pexels-cristian-rojas-8853388.jpg" fig-alt="Photo of a woman and a man discussing a project."  background-size="cover" background-position="bottom" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

::: {.notes}
*Timing: ~ 21 min in by here.*

So, I've laid some foundations by defining missing data and discussing why it 
matters. We know it poses measurement, internal validity, and generalizability 
problems for our studies. Now the next logical question is "How should we start 
addressing missing data issues?" To answer that, I'm going to introduce a
framework that can guide your efforts to do that.
:::

## TARMOS Framework [@Lee-RN8488]

**T**reatment **A**nd **R**eporting of **M**issing data in **O**bservational 
**S**tudies

* Advocates for systematic, transparent methodology
* Recommends a 3-step approach
* Principles are applicable beyond observational studies

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 21-22 min by here.*

The Treatment And Reporting of Missing data in Observational Studies (TARMOS)
framework advocates for applying a systematic, transparent methodology for
handling missing data. It recommends taking a 3-step approach to that task. It
is grounded in a few principles that I think are applicable both to the
observational research designs that were the focus of TARMOS and to other
research designs such as experimental and quasi-experimental studies.

Next, I'll briefly describe the steps in TARMOS, along with one small adjustment 
I would make to the framework. 
:::
::::

## TARMOS Step 1: Plan the Analysis

* Identify the analyses you would use if there were no missing data
    * Write a statistical analysis plan [@Cressman-RN8487]
* Decide how you will handle missing data 
    * Is a complete records analysis likely to be valid?
    * Is multiple imputation likely to offer benefits over a complete records 
      analysis?
    * Is a sensitivity analysis required, and if so how should it be framed?

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 22-23 min by here.*

The first step in TARMOS encourages you to start by identifying the analysis 
models you would use if there were no missing data at all. Here, it can be 
useful to apply recommendations about writing good statistical analysis plans. 
Well-chosen analyses will describe your sample, answer your research questions,
and test your hypotheses while properly accounting for the research design and
relevant covariates. A full specification of required analyses reveals the set 
of variables that *must be available* to achieve your scientific objectives. 
Missing data on those *essential variables* pose problems; missing data on 
*other variables* may be irrelevant to your study.

TARMOS step 1 also calls for deciding how you will handle missing data. It asks 
you to consider a few key questions, such as: 

* Is a complete records analysis likely to be valid?
* Is multiple imputation likely to offer benefits over a complete records 
  analysis?
* Is a sensitivity analysis required, and if so how should it be framed?

Understanding both concepts from the missing data literature and various methods
that *could* be applied facilitates making decisions. You can identify some 
general options during step 1 but my opinion is that the actual choice should be 
made in step 2.
:::
::::

## TARMOS Step 2: Conduct the Analysis

* Examine whether data are suitable for planned analysis
    * Apply initial data analysis guidelines 
      [@Baillie-RN8311; @Heinze-RN8641; @Huebner-RN3947; @Huebner-RN3997]
    * Diagnose missing data issues 
      [@Enders-RN8673; @Enders-RN8974; @McKnight-RN1296] *before* deciding how 
      to handle them
    * Update the statistical analysis plan
* Conduct substantive analysis, acknowledge & justify deviations from the plan

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 24-25 min by here.*

The second step in TARMOS is conducting analyses. The framework encourages you
to examine whether your data are suitable for the planned analysis. There is a
growing literature with guidelines about planning and conducting initial data
analyses that accomplish this purpose. One key message is that the initial data
analyses *do not address the substantive research questions and hypotheses*.
They focus on assessing whether it is feasible to conduct the substantive
analysis as originally planned or you will instead have to adapt your analysis
plan to deal with unexpected features of the data.

Here, I want to emphasize that a thorough diagnosis of the missing data issues
in your data should be part of your initial data analysis. Those results give
you the rest of the information you need to make good decisions about handling
missing data in your substantive analyses. If you are lucky, there won't be any
missing data at all and you can just implement your statistical analysis plan
as-is. However, the diagnostic results may show you that the planned analysis
will not yield trustworthy answers unless you modify it to handle the missing
data sensibly.

So my take is that TARMOS should have recommended making the decision 
about how to handle missing data in the middle of step 2. 
*Update the analysis plan after reviewing the missing data diagnostics!* 

After that, finish TARMOS step 2 by conducting your substantive analysis. It is 
important to acknowledge and justify any deviations from the original analysis 
plan. The initial data analyses should provide evidence for why those changes 
were necessary. 
:::
::::

## TARMOS Step 3: Report the Analysis

* Describe the missing data
* Describe how missing data were handled & justify the approach
* Report & interpret results

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 26-27 min by here.*

The third and final step in TARMOS is reporting your analysis. Your paper should
describe the missing data issues you detected in Step 2, how you handled them,
and why you selected that approach. Obviously, you should then include and
interpret the results of your analyses and any sensitivity analyses or
robustness checks that you conducted.

So, TARMOS can be distilled down to three words: plan, conduct, and report. 
:::
::::

# How can we diagnose the missing data issues for a given study?  {style="padding: 0.25em .5em; background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, .5); backdrop-filter: blur(5px); box-shadow: 0 0 1rem 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .5); border-radius: 20px; text-align: left;" background-image="graphics/pexels-fauxels-3184634.jpg" fig-alt="Photo of a team of several people in business suits sitting at a table discussing a report."  background-size="cover" background-position="bottom" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 27-28 min by here.*

So, I've laid some foundations by outlining the TARMOS framework, but each study
is unique. The nature and extent of the missing data issues you face will vary
across studies. We can't make good choices about what to do about missing data
until we thoroughly understand the problems on a given study. The next logical
question is "how can we diagnose the missing data issues for a given study?"

To answer that, I'm going to cover some additional concepts that pertain to
describing the amounts, patterns, and mechanisms of missing data. It helps to
start by thinking about data structures that affect how we organize and use
data.
:::
::::

## Cattell's Data Box [@Cattell-RN8693] {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

``` {r}
#| label: data-box
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 4.5
#| fig-height: 4.5

options(rgl.useNULL=TRUE)
open3d(silent = TRUE)
cube3d(scaleMatrix(5,5,5), col = "#C3FFEC", quads = 5) |> 
  translate3d(1, 1, 1) |> 
  shade3d()

axis3d(edge = 'x--', labels = c("1", "2", ".", ".", ".", "V"))
mtext3d(text = "Variable", edge = 'x++', line = 4, floating = TRUE)

axis3d(edge = 'y-+', labels = c("1", "2", ".", ".", ".", "T"))
mtext3d(text = "Time", edge = 'y++', at = 6, line = 5, floating = TRUE)

axis3d(edge = 'z--', labels = c("P", ".", ".", ".", "2", "1"))
mtext3d(text = "Person", edge = 'z++', line = 4, floating = TRUE)

view3d(theta = 0, phi = -60, zoom = .9, interactive = FALSE)
grid3d(side = c("x+", "y-", "z+"), col = "black")
rglwidget()
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<br> *How much data is there? Data volume is* $N_{values} = P \times V \times T$

* Slices through the cube represent subsets of data
* Constructs are often measured by groups of adjacent variables (items)
* Missing data puts holes in your cube!

:::

::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 28-29 min by here.*

Raymond Cattell described data as forming a 3-dimensional box or cube, with the
dimensions being persons, variables, and occasions (or times). This is useful
for thinking about the total volume of data we aim to collect. For a simple
longitudinal study where we collect the same instruments for each person at each
time point, we can just multiply the number of desired participants by the
number of variables by the number of time points to get the number of desired
data values. That's the volume of the cube. Different slices through the cube
represent specific subsets of data. The top layer would be all the data for a
given person (all variables at all time points). The left-most vertical slice
would be all the data for a specific variable across all persons and time
points. The front face of the cube would be time 1 data on all variables for all
persons. We could further refine this idea by recognizing that constructs are
often measured by groups of adjacent variables (items).

Missing data puts holes in the cube, turning it into something like Swiss 
cheese. The impact of that depends on how much data volume is lost and any 
patterns in where and why those holes are located relative to the data you want 
to analyze. 
:::
::::

## Slicing & Rearranging the Data Cube

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 9
//| fig-height: 5

digraph CutCube {
rankdir="TB";

node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Cube [label = "Data Cube\nP x V x T"]
Slices [label = "T Slices\n P x V"]
Long [label = "Long Univariate Format\nTime → Extra Rows\n1 case per person-period"]
Wide [label = "Wide Multivariate Format\nTime → Extra Columns\n1 case per person"]

edge [fontname="Lato"]
Cube -> Slices [label = "Cut By Time"]
Slices -> Long [label = "Stack"]
Slices -> Wide [label = "Spread"]
}
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 29-31 min by here.*

The 3-dimensional data cube concept is useful, but actual data files are often
constrained to a 2-dimensional matrix with rows for cases and columns for 
variables. We have to slice and rearrange the box to handle the time dimension
sensibly. There are two common formats for representing longitudinal or repeated
measures data. 

The long univariate format keeps all data about a given variable
in a single column, so each time point must become a distinct set of rows. Each
person ends up with one row per time point because we are vertically stacking
the time slices.

The wide multivariate format keeps all data about a given person in a single
row, so each time point must become an additional set of columns. We are
spreading the time slices out horizontally and renaming variables as needed
along the way.
::: 
::::

## Data Structure: Long

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-LONG
#| tbl-cap: Long Univariate Format

set.seed(24687)

LONG <- expand.grid(ID = 1:3, Time = 1:2) |>  
  mutate(X1 = sample(x = 11:33, size = 6, replace = TRUE), 
         X2 = sample(x = 1:7, size = 6, replace = TRUE),
         X3 = sample(x = letters[1:3], size = 6, replace = TRUE)) |>  
  arrange(ID, Time)

kable(LONG, format = "html", align = "c") |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                lightable_options = c("hover"), html_font = "Lato") 
```

::::

:::: {.column width="10%"}
::::

:::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

<br><br><br> <!-- Add line breaks to control layout-->

Cube: $P = 3, V = 3, T = 2$, $N_{cases} = P \times T = 6$

* Including ID & Time: $V = 5$, $N_{values} = P \times V \times T = 30$
* Excluding ID & Time: $V = 3$, $N_{values} = P \times V \times T = 18$
    
:::
::::
:::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 31-33 min by here.*

Suppose our data cube has measurements for P = 3 people, on three variables (X1,
X2, and X3), each measured at two time points. That implies a total of 18
values. This table is the result of translating that into a long univariate
format. We have to add two structural variables (ID and Time) that should never
have any missing data and are used to uniquely identify a row of data belonging
to a specific person at a specific time point. We end up with 6 cases and 5 
variables, for a total of 30 possible data values, of which 18 are the contents 
of the original data cube.
:::
::::

## Data Structure: Wide

```{r}
#| label: tbl-WIDE
#| tbl-cap: Wide Multivariate Format

WIDE <- LONG |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Time, values_from = c(X1, X2, X3), 
              names_glue = "T{Time}_{.value}", names_vary = "slowest")

kable(WIDE, format = "html", align = "c") |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                lightable_options = c("hover"), html_font = "Lato") |> 
  add_header_above(header = c(" ", "Time 1" = 3, "Time 2" = 3))
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.7em;"}
Cube: $P = 3, V = 3 , T = 2$. $N_{cases} = P = 3$

* Including ID: $V = 7$, $N_{values} = P \times V = 21$
* Excluding ID: $V = 6$, $N_{values} = P \times V = 18$

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 32-34 min by here.*

This table shows the same data you saw on the last slide, rearranged into wide
format. So, now we have one row per unique ID, but we need six variables to
represent X1, X2, and X3 at the two time points. I added a time prefix to each
variable name, and grouped the variables by time point. The column grouping
labels above the variable names just clarify that structure. We now have a total
of 21 values, of which 18 are the contents of the original cube.
:::
::::

## Choosing Data Structure

* Long & wide data sets are useful for different diagnostic tasks
* Pay attention to which structure you are using
* You may need *both* long & wide data sets
* Use the *relevant subset* of cases & variables in diagnostics

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 32-34 min by here.*

Both the long and wide data structures are useful but assessing some kinds of
missing data issues may be easier with one structure than with the other.
Naturally, you should pay attention to which data structure you are using and
think about whether it facilitates or hinders your diagnostic work. You may find
that you actually need *both* long and wide data sets, so be prepared to 
restructure your data from one format to the other.

In any case, review your analysis plan to identify the subsets of cases and 
variables that are relevant to your planned analyses. Those must be included in
the diagnostic analyses, but we often have more data than is strictly required 
for a given substantive analysis. Create smaller data sets for use in the 
diagnostic analyses. 
:::
::::

## Types of Missingness [@McKnight-RN1296; @Schafer-RN425]

* Item level
* Construct level
* Person level (unit non-response)
* Person-period level (wave nonresponse; intermittent vs. dropout)

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 34-36 min by here.*

There are a variety of types of missingness to consider as we begin describing 
a given data set. One place to start involves examining different parts of the
data cube. For example, item-level missingness pertains to an individual 
variable. What percentage of its values are missing? Contrast that with 
construct-level missingness, which is when an entire construct is missing. Some 
scoring methods leave a construct missing if any of its items are missing, 
others may still yield a score despite some missing items. Construct missingness 
is usually a bigger problem than item-level missing data. 

Person-level missing data, some times called unit nonresponse, is when all the
data for a person is missing. Refusing to participate will usually cause
person-level missing data. That's an extreme example, but a related type of
missingness happens at the person-period level when a participant skips an
entire data collection event. That's also called wave non-response. It affects
all constructs collected at that time point. In longitudinal studies,
person-period missingness could be intermittent, with a participant skipping one
event but returning for subsequent events, or it may be a result of dropout or
attrition that affects all remaining time points.
:::
::::

## Example Data: Size & Missingness Overview

```{r}
#| label: tbl-CVV-Missing
#| tbl-cap: "Missingness Overview for Dutch Boys Growth Study Data [@Fredriks-RN8696]"
#| results: asis

FN <- paste("<small>", 
            "Cross-sectional data (wide multivariate format).",
            "</small>")
  
cvv_missingness(boys) |> 
  kable(format = "html", align = "r", digits = 1,
        col.names = c("Subset", rep(c("n", "%"), times = 3))) |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                lightable_options = c("hover"), html_font = "Lato") |>
  row_spec(row = 3, italic = TRUE) |> 
  add_header_above(header = c(" " = 1, "Cases" = 2, "Variables" = 2, 
                              "Values" = 2)) |> 
  footnote(general = FN, general_title = "<small>Note: </small>", 
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, escape = F, threeparttable = TRUE) |> 
  cat()
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 36-38 min by here.*

Here I'm showing a table with a missingness overview for data from a study of 
growth among Dutch boys. This is cross-sectional data, which is effectively in 
a wide multivariate format. That means each boy is represented by a single case.
The bottom row shows the size of the dataset: We have 748 boys and measurements 
for 9 variables, yielding a total of 6732 possible values. 

This table shows us that 70% of the cases are incomplete, meaning those boys
each have at least one variable with a missing value on their row of data. We
can also see that 8 of the 9 variables are incomplete, meaning at least one boy
has missing data in that column. Finally, the Values columns show us that about
24% of all the possible values in the data set are missing. So a quarter of the 
volume of the data cube is empty.

This sort of overview provides crucial diagnostic information. For example, if
the Incomplete row were to contain only zeroes, you would immediately know there
is no missing data and no further diagnostics are required.
:::
::::

## Comparing MD Overviews for Long vs Wide Data

```{r}
#| label: tbl-WIDEvsLONG
#| tbl-cap: Meaning of Incomplete Cases and Variables By Data Set Format

LvW <- data.frame(format = c("Wide", "Long"), 
                  cases = c("Persons with any MD (across variables & times)", 
                            "Persons with any MD at specific times (across variables)"),
                  variables = c("Variables with any MD at specific times (across persons)", 
                                "Variables with any MD (across persons & times)"))

kable(LvW, format = "html", align = "c", 
      col.names = c("Format", 
                    paste("Incomplete", c("Cases", "Variables")))) |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                lightable_options = c("hover"), html_font = "Lato") 
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 37-39 min by here.*

If you have longitudinal data, the meaning of incomplete cases and incomplete 
variables depends on whether you are summarizing a wide dataset or a long one. 
This table shows some key points about interpreting the type of missingness 
overview from the last slide. Notice that:

* For wide formats, incomplete cases are persons with any missing data at all 
  across both all variables and all times, while the incomplete variables are 
  time-specific. 
* Meanwhile, for long formats, incomplete cases are persons with any missing 
  data at specific times but across all variables.  That means it is identifying 
  person-periods with missing data. Incomplete variables in long format data 
  sets are those with any missing data at all across all persons and all times. 

So, running these overviews for both long and wide versions of longitudinal
datasets reveals additional insights about the amount of missing data. It is
easy to do. Some statistical software packages have really nice features for
producing these summaries and other missing data diagnostics.
:::
::::

## Example Data: Variable-Level Missingness 

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-Var-Missing
#| tbl-cap: "Item/Construct Missingness for Dutch Boys Growth Study Data [@Fredriks-RN8696]"
#| results: asis

FN <- paste("<small>", 
            "Cross-sectional data (wide multivariate format).",
            "</small>")
  
var_missingness(boys) |> 
  select(-Position, -N_Total) |> 
  kable(format = "html", align = "r", digits = 1,
        col.names = c("Variable", rep(c("n", "%"), times = 2))) |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
                lightable_options = c("hover"), html_font = "Lato", 
                font_size = 25) |> 
  row_spec(row = 2:3, italic = TRUE) |> 
  row_spec(row = 4, bold = TRUE) |> 
  add_header_above(header = c(" ", "Observed" = 2, "Missing" = 2)) |> 
  footnote(general = FN, general_title = "<small>Note: </small>", 
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, escape = F, threeparttable = TRUE) |> 
  cat()
```

::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
<br><br> <!-- Add line breaks to control layout-->

::: {.incremental}
* Age is a 1-item construct
* Body mass index is a **2-item construct**: 
    * $bmi = wgt/hgt^2$
    * Both *items* are required
:::
::::
:::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 39-41 min by here.*

The missingness overviews tell you how many variables have missing data, but not
which variables have it or how much is missing for each one. That's why you
should also examine a table of variable-level missingness statistics. This table
shows one for the example dataset I used a couple slides ago. This can reveal
both item and construct missingness. A variable may represent an item, a
construct, or both.

* ▶ Here age is a single-item construct that has no missing data
* ▶ and body mass index is a 2-item construct defined by 
* ▶ the ratio of weight divided by height squared. 
* ▶ Both variables are required to compute bmi, so bmi will be missing 
  whenever either weight or height is missing. 

Nevertheless, bmi still has a low rate of missingness. In contrast, the 
variables gen, phb, and tv--which are all measures of pubertal development--have 
very high rates of missingness. The importance of handling missing data well
increases with the rate of missingness for variables required by your planned
analyses.

Consider running a variable-level missingness summary like this for both long
and wide format datasets if you have longitudinal data. That may be especially
important if your planned analysis will only use a subset of the available time
points.
:::
::::

## Describing the Amount of MD [@McKnight-RN1296; @van-Buuren-RN3962]

Report numbers & percentages of: 

:::{.incremental}
* Incomplete cases, variables, & values (overall & by time)
* Missing values per variable/construct (overall & by time)
* Participants w/ *any observed data* at each time point (retention/attendance)
* Reasons for dropout and other types of missing data

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 41-43 min by here.*

To comply with TARMOS step 3, research products such as manuscripts should
include some obvious summaries of the amount of missing data, such as the
numbers and percentages of:

* ▶ Incomplete cases, variables, and values, like the overviews I 
  showed earlier, both overall and by time point.
* ▶ Missing values for each variable and construct, both overall and by time 
  point.
* ▶ Participants with *any observed data* at each time point. That helps you 
  examine retention & attendance rates in longitudinal studies. 
* ▶ Reasons for why participants drop out, skip data collection events, 
  or have other missing data. 
  
:::
::::

## Tracking Systems
Use a tracking system to organize & document data collection

::: {.incremental}
* Whether & when participants attend each data collection event
* When, how, & why participants exit the study

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 42-44 min by here.*

I strongly recommend designing and using a tracking system that organizes and 
documents your data collection activities. It should aim to record things like: 

* ▶ Whether & when participants attended data each collection event.
* ▶ When, how, & why participants exit the study, whether that's because they 
  completed everything as planned, or because they withdrew partway through, 
  you lost contact, or perhaps even became ineligible for further participation
  because of a change in personal circumstances. 

Such a tracking system provides you with data you can analyze to get crucial 
insights into missing data issues. 

:::
::::

## CONSORT 2025 Statement [@Hopewell-RN9004]

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
* Requires disclosing how you handled MD
* Flow charting sample size by stage of study:
    * Uses tracking data
    * Reveals dropout

:::

::: {.column width="60%"}

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 6
//| fig-height: 5

digraph CONSORT {
rankdir="TB";

node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Assessed [label = "n, Assessed for eligibility"]
Randomized [label = "n, Randomly assigned"]
Assigned1 [label = "Arm 1 (Intervention)\nn, Assigned"]
Assigned2 [label = "Arm 2 (Control)\nn, Assigned"]
Dropped1 [label = "Dropped\nn, Discontinued arm (give reasons)\nn, Lost to follow-up (give reasons)"]
Dropped2 [label = "Dropped\nn, Discontinued arm (give reasons)\nn, Lost to follow-up (give reasons)"]
Analyzed1 [label = "Primary outcome analysis\nn, Included\nn, Excluded (give reasons)"]
Analyzed2 [label = "Primary outcome analysis\nn, Included\nn, Excluded (give reasons)"]

Assessed -> Randomized
Randomized -> Assigned1 -> Dropped1 -> Analyzed1
Randomized -> Assigned2 -> Dropped2 -> Analyzed2
}
```

:::
::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 43-45 min by here.*

The Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (or CONSORT) 2025 statement is a 
set of reporting guidelines specific to studies designed as randomized 
controlled trials. A number of journals require authors to show they have met 
the CONSORT checklist requirements when submitting papers for such studies. 

Among other things, these standards require disclosing how you handled missing
data. They include creating a flow chart like this one that shows how your
sample size changes across stages of the study from assessing eligibility all
the way to analyzing your outcomes. This is particularly useful for identifying
when and why participants drop out. Obviously, dropping out early is a major 
cause of missing data in longitudinal studies. 

So the lesson here is that designing your tracking system to collect the data 
required to fill out a CONSORT chart is a prudent investment. 
:::
::::

## Patterns of Missing Data [@Schafer-RN425]

::: {.incremental}

* **Y**: matrix of all the values that *could* be observed 
* **Y_obs**: subset of *observed* **Y** values 
* **Y_miss**: subset of *missing* **Y** values
* **R**: response matrix of dummy-coded missingness indicators showing which 
  **Y** values are observed (0, part of **Y_obs**) vs. missing (1, part of 
  **Y_miss**)
  
:::

:::: {.fragment}

::: {.callout-tip}
We can aggregrate and visualize **R** to describe patterns of missingness!
:::

::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 44-46 min by here.*

The amount of missing data is crucial, but the patterns of which data values are 
missing are also important diagnostic tools. Now I need to define a few terms 
and symbols that are useful for describing patterns. 

* ▶  Suppose Y is a complete matrix of all the cases, variables, and values 
that could be observed. We can split it into two pieces, 
* ▶  Y_obs which is the subset of Y that ends up being observed and 
* ▶  Y_miss is the subset that ends up missing. 
* ▶  We can then define a response matrix called R that has a set of 
  dummy-coded missingess indicators that use a 0 to denote which Y values are 
  observed and part of Y_obs, and a 1 for those that are missing and belong to 
  Y_miss instead. R has the same number of variables as Y.

▶  Creating R gives us some ways to examine missing data issues. For example, 
we can aggregate across cases to identify unique patterns of missing vs observed
values and see how often they occur.
:::
::::

## Example Data: MD Patterns Plot

```{r}
#| label: example-patterns
#| fig-cap: "Missingness patterns for Dutch boys growth study data (748 boys, 9 
#|           variables, 1 time point) [@Fredriks-RN8696]"

plot_pattern(boys, square = FALSE, rotate = FALSE)
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 45-47 min by here.*

This graph shows missing data patterns in the example growth data about Dutch
boys obtained by aggregating the R matrix. Each row is a distinct pattern of
observed values (in blue cells) versus missing values (in pink cells) across the
whole set of variables. Columns are variables, sorted by number of missing
values. This shows some things we learned from the overview and variable-level
missingness tables, plus new details those simpler tables could not reveal.

The left side lists the number of boys in each pattern, the right side lists how
many missing values are on that row. The bottom shows the number of missing
values for each variable. We know there should be 748 boys, 9 variables, and
6732 values. A footnote at the bottom shows that 1622 values (24%) are missing.

The first row shows 223 boys (30%) have complete data. The 70% of boys with
incomplete data are divided into 12 additional patterns, but 8 of them are very
rare, affecting three or fewer boys. The most common pattern has 437 boys with
missing data for all 3 pubertal development measures, but observed data on all
the other measures. That's 58% of the sample.

Another thing we can see here is the construct missingness in body mass index 
that we know is caused by item-missingness in height or weight. 
:::
::::

## Using MD Pattern Plots

Look for patterns that: 

:::{.incremental}

* Are common (i.e., high frequency)
* Have large numbers of missing values
* Relate item missingness to construct missingness
* Show entire data collection events being skipped
* Show intermittent attendance or dropout

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 47-49 min by here.*

As with the missing data overview and variable-level missingness summaries, you
may want to use missing data pattern plots on both long and wide versions of
your data. Both overall and time-specific versions may be useful. These plots,
especially in conjunction with data from a good tracking system, can help you
discover things you can use later in handling missing data problems. It takes
some creativity and thought to use them well, but some things you might look for
include patterns that:

* ▶  Are common (i.e., they occur with high frequency).
* ▶  Have large numbers of missing values.
* ▶  Relate item missingness to construct missingness.
* ▶  Show entire data collection events being skipped.
* ▶  Show intermittent attendance or dropout. 

:::
::::

## Rubin's Mechanisms of Missingness [@Rubin-RN8695]

:::{.incremental}

* Missing completely at random (MCAR)
* Missing at random (MAR)
* Missing not at random (MNAR)

:::

::: {.fragment}

:::{.callout-caution title = "Impact on Statistical Results [@McKnight-RN1296]"}
Some mechanisms yield more bias: **MCAR < MAR < MNAR**
:::

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 48-50 min by here.*

Understanding Donald Rubin's system for classifying missing data is crucial to
making good decisions about how to handle missing data. This system is pervasive
in the literature on missing data because it's useful despite reliance on some
unintuitive names. Rubin defined three categories, each referring to the
probability of missing values given information about the variables with missing
data, other covariates, and a hypothetical mechanism that causes the missing
data. The three categories are:

* ▶ Missing completely at random (MCAR)
* ▶ Missing at random (MAR)
* ▶ Missing not at random (MNAR)

▶  These three mechanisms affect the amount of bias missing data causes in 
statistical analyses, with MCAR yielding the least bias and MNAR yielding the 
most. Let's take a closer look at each mechanism. 
:::
::::

## MCAR
MCAR is when neither observed nor unobserved values predict which values are 
missing

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 9
//| fig-height: 4

digraph MCAR {
rankdir="LR";

node [shape = "ellipse", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Missing [label = "Missingness\n(R)"]
Observed [label = "Observed Values\n(Y_obs)", style = "dashed"]
Random [label = "Random Processes\nUnrelated to Y"]
Unobserved [label = "Unobserved Values\n(Y_miss)", style = "dashed"]

edge [fontname="Lato"]
Random -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Unobserved -> Random -> Observed [style = "invis"]

{rank = same; Unobserved; Random; Observed}
}
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 49-51 min by here.*

MCAR is the least damaging kind of missingness because it means that neither
observed nor unobserved values predict which values are likely to be missing.
The missingness is entirely a result of random processes: there's no discernible
pattern to it. That's why it causes the least bias in statistical analyses.
However, using listwise deletion will still reduce measurement reliability and
statistical power and precision with MCAR data. There is a formal statistical
test for whether MCAR is a plausible mechanism. I don't see it used very often,
but that's partly because few papers fully report missing data diagnostics.
:::
::::

## MAR
MAR is when observed values predict which values are missing

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 9
//| fig-height: 4

digraph MAR {
rankdir="LR";

node [shape = "ellipse", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Missing [label = "Missingness\n(R)"]
Observed [label = "Observed Values\n(Y_obs)"]
Random [label = "Random Processes\nUnrelated to Y"]
Unobserved [label = "Unobserved Values\n(Y_miss)", style = "dashed"]

edge [fontname="Lato"]
Observed -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Unobserved -> Random -> Observed [style = "invis"]
Random -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Unobserved -> Missing [style = "invis"]

{rank = same; Unobserved; Random; Observed}
}
```


::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 50-52 min by here.*

MAR is when observed values predict which values are likely to be missing. Thus,
we can see patterns in what's missing because they are related to data that we
have available. While there may still be some random influences too, the crucial
feature of MAR is that the probability of missingess depends on observed data
*but not on the unobserved, missing data itself*. MAR is sometimes called
ignorable nonresponse [@Schafer-RN425], but that ignorability requires proper
handling of the missing data during analyses. We have to account for the
relationships between missingness and observed values to reduce bias. This
mechanism is plausible for many datasets. You can run analyses to examine
whether MAR is more plausible than MCAR.
:::
::::

## MNAR
MNAR is when unobserved values predict which values are missing

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 9
//| fig-height: 4

digraph MNAR {
rankdir="LR";

node [shape = "ellipse", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
Missing [label = "Missingness\n(R)"]
Observed [label = "Observed Values\n(Y_obs)"]
Random [label = "Random Processes\nUnrelated to Y"]
Unobserved [label = "Unobserved Values\n(Y_miss)"]

edge [fontname="Lato"]
Unobserved -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Random -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Observed -> Missing [label = "Predict"]
Unobserved -> Random -> Observed [style = "invis"]

{rank = same; Unobserved; Random; Observed}
}
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 51-53 min by here.*

MNAR is sometimes called non-ignorable nonresponse [@Schafer-RN425] because
under this mechanism whether or not a value is missing depends on unobserved
values, either on the variable itself, or on other unobserved variables. There
are hidden patterns to the missingness that we can't easily account for in our
analyses. That leads to potentially serious biases in our results. 

There are researchers who have proposed methods that can generate clues that 
MNAR mechanisms may be in play [@Weberpals-RN8957], but we can't definitely
prove our data is MNAR without actually obtaining the data that are missing.
However, if we could get them, then we wouldn't even have missing data anymore.
You may be able to make logical arguments for why we might expect data to be
MNAR in a specific study, but other scientists may or may not be persuaded by
those arguments when you can't provide supporting evidence due to lack of the
necessary data.
:::
::::

## Rubin's Mechanisms & Cattell's Box [@Cattell-RN8693; @McKnight-RN1296; @Rubin-RN8695]

Classifying large datasets according to Rubin's mechanisms is *messy*

::: {.incremental}
* Subsets of the data may fit into different mechanisms
* Classify mechanisms for meaningful subsets of data defined by dimensions of 
  the data box (persons, variables, times) [@McKnight-RN1296] 
* Consider structured approaches to diagnosing mechanisms [@Weberpals-RN8957]
:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 52-54 min by here.*

McKnight and colleagues pointed out that classfying large datasets into Rubin's 
mechanisms is a practical challenge because it just gets messy. The task can 
feel overwhelming. 

* ▶  It can be daunting because variables may be subject to different 
  missingness mechanisms. One set of variables might really be MCAR while others 
  are MAR. 
* ▶  One way to start solving that problem is to use the dimensions of 
  Cattell's box (persons, variables, and times) to describe missing data and 
  classify missingness mechanisms in meaningful subsets of the data. 
* ▶ Consider structured approaches to diagnosing mechanisms [@Weberpals-RN8957].

:::
::::

## Predictors of Missingness in Longitudinal Studies

::: {.incremental}
* Study arms and/or sites
* Baseline/pretest values of outcome variables 
* Other covariates (demographics)

:::

:::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
Consider predictors of person, item, construct, and person-period missingness. 
Think carefully about your study context and data to look for meaningful, 
sensible things to test when evaluating missing data issues. 
:::
::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 52-55 min by here.*

Because we can't prove that our missing data is MNAR, one of the key tasks is 
differentiating between data that are MCAR and MAR. To do that, run analyses 
that test possible predictors of which values are missing. The R matrix columns 
are the outcome variables in such models. Here are some examples.

* ▶ If you have an experiment with multiple groups (like intervention and 
  control arms) or sites, compare consent, attendance, and attrition rates 
  between them. If arm or site predicts such rates, you may have a MAR 
  situation. 
* ▶ Check whether baseline or pretest values of the outcome measures predict
  missingness of the same outcomes at later times. People with unusually high or 
  low scores may be more likely to end up with missing data later.
* ▶ Demographics and other covariates may also be predictors of missing data. 
* ▶ So the message here is that your initial data analysis should include 
  testing for predictors of missingness. That may be a matter of predicting 
  either person-level non-response (refusal to participate), item or 
  construct-level missingness, or person-period missingness, such as attrition 
  or intermittently skipping data collection events. Finding predictors of 
  missingness means that you have a MAR situation. 
  
:::
::::

# Audience Questions (2) 
Do you have questions about diagnosing missing data issues? 

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 53-56 min by here. Continue by ~ 58-61 (0:58-1:01)*

Let's pause to see if there are any questions about diagnosing missing data 
issues? 
:::
::::

# How should we solve missing data issues? {style="padding: 0.25em .5em; background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, .5); backdrop-filter: blur(5px); box-shadow: 0 0 1rem 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, .5); border-radius: 20px; text-align: left;" background-image="graphics/pexels-kampus-8463151.jpg" fig-alt="Photo of four people in a meeting."  background-size="cover" background-position="bottom" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

:::{.notes}
*Timing: ~ 58-61 min (0:59-1:01) by here.*

The million-dollar question is ultimately "how should we solve missing data 
issues?" Let's discuss that next. 
:::

## Prevention [@de-Leeuw-RN8682; @McKnight-RN1296; @Wisniewski-RN2978]  {style="text-align: right;" background-image="graphics/boston-public-library-ts_mJIUonBY-unsplash.jpg" background-size="contain" background-position="left" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
"An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure." (Benjamin Franklin, 1735)

* The amount of missing data affects its potential impact on the study  
* Design your study to reduce the amount of missing data

:::
::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 59-61 min (0:59-1:01) by here.*

As the old adage often attributed to Benjamin Franklin says, "An ounce of
prevention is worth a pound of cure." This absolutely applies to missing data!

Statistical methods for analyzing incomplete datasets are great, but using them 
requires knowledge, skill, software, time, and effort. The size and complexity 
of the missing data problem is directly related to its potential impact on your 
study and what it will take to solve that problem.  

Fortunately, good study design can help researchers reduce the probability and
amount of missing data, thereby reducing its potential impact on measurement,
internal validity, and generalizability. The prevention suggestions I'm going to
share are not an exhaustive list. I just want you to recognize that prevention
is your first, and maybe best, opportunity to handle missing data problems.
:::
::::

## Prevention: Data Collection Frequency & Timing [@McKnight-RN1296] 

:::{.incremental}
* Frequent data collection with short intervals between events is burdensome
* Collect longitudinal data at sensible times given expected temporal patterns
:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 60-62 min (1:00-1:02) by here.*

First, let's consider fine-tuning data collection logistics. 

▶ When participants have to put effort into responding, frequent data collection
at short intervals increases their burden. That risks decreasing their
motivation and the effort put into any given response, which could cause item,
construct, or wave non-response, or even lead to dropout.

▶ While having rich longitudinal data is good for modeling, it is better to have
more complete data collected at the right times than to have many time points.
Often we have an idea about the temporal patterns of change that we expect, so
focus on the critical periods that enable you to see the key aspects of
longitudinal trajectories. Collect more often during periods where you expect
rapid change and less often when you expect relative stability or slower change.
:::
::::

## Prevention: Number of Variables [@de-Leeuw-RN8682; @McKnight-RN1296; @Wisniewski-RN2978] 

Every variable is an opportunity for missing data

:::{.incremental}
* Only collect variables that you need
* Choose instruments wisely (short, reliable, valid, & relevant)
* Consider collecting multiple measures of key constructs

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 61-63 min (1:01-1:03) by here.*

Remember that every variable you try to collect adds to participant burden. 
Asking for too much risks tiring, frustrating, boring, or irritating people in 
ways that lead to missing data. 

▶ You can reduce missing data problems by only collecting variables you need. You 
should know why you need each variable or instrument. 

▶ Similarly, you should choose instruments wisely: pick things that are short,
reliable, and valid whenever possible. Making sure their relevance is clear to
participants may also help because people may be more likely to skip things they
think are irrelevant or offensive.

▶ On the other hand, there is a case for strategically collecting multiple
measures of key constructs. Different measures of a construct are often
correlated and that can help you if data for one are incomplete but another is
complete. Relationships between measures can be used to recover some of the
missing information when modeling incomplete datasets.
:::
::::

## Prevention: Dropout [@de-Leeuw-RN8682; @Laurie-RN8257; @McKnight-RN1296; @Wisniewski-RN2978] 

:::{.incremental}
* Establish relationships 
* Maintain contact with participants 
* Offer adequate incentives
* Make participation convenient

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 62-64 min (1:02-1:04) by here.*

Dropout is a major cause of missing data in longitudinal studies, but there are
strategies you can use to minimize it. I'll describe some that we use on an 
evaluation of a federal nutrition education program for low-income people.

* ▶ We partner with local organizations to find participants; good relationships 
  with them means they help us keep participants engaged. 
* ▶ Another key strategy is maintaining contact with participants. Reminder
  postcards, text messages, and phone calls encourage returning for follow-up 
  data collection. If someone skips one event, we still invite them to the
  next one unless they explicitly withdraw from the study. Intermittent wave
  non-response is better than complete dropout.
* ▶ We also aim to offer people adequate incentives to participate. We pay 
  participants promptly for each data collection event, with the amounts 
  increasing over time. 
* ▶ Finally, we make data collection convenient by scheduling events at places
  and times participants can easily attend, or by scheduling extra sessions. We 
  send multiple data collectors to each session so it runs efficiently and 
  doesn't waste participants' time. For some groups, we use translated 
  instruments and staff that speak participants' primary language.

:::
::::

## Prevention: Data Collection Methods

:::{.incremental}
* Develop detailed protocols
* Train data collection & entry staff 
* Pay attention to instrument design 
* Test equipment & instruments

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 64-66 min (1:04-1:06) by here.*

* ▶ Developing detailed study protocols can help maximize data quality. They
  should include elements that can prevent missing data. For example, reviewing 
  checklists of required materials before each data collection event helps 
  ensure you take what you need. If you're administering print surveys in a 
  group setting, check each one for skipped items or sections as it's handed in
  while there's still a chance for the participant to respond. If you're using 
  an online survey, program it to notify participants when they skip something 
  and give them the option to either fill it in or proceed anyway. If you're 
  going to observe a classroom, consider using multiple observers and structured 
  forms for recording data. Define the behaviors to be recorded. Keep 
  observation sessions short enough to avoid fatigue and inattention. Similar 
  ideas apply if you're going to review documents or abstract data from records: 
  use multiple staff, structured forms, and clear definitions.
* ▶ Train your staff on the protocols and get them to practice before 
  collecting real data. For data entry, plan a review process and give staff 
  performance feedback. Consider using double-entry and reconciling discrepancies 
  between the data entered by 2 different staff.
* ▶ Poorly designed instruments may increase the rate of missing data. Pay close
  attention to instrument design. Make sure instructions and wording are clear,
  layout is readable, response options make sense, and follow principles of good 
  survey design. Pilot test the instruments.
* ▶ Make sure you test equipment regularly, have spare devices, cords, or 
  batteries in case one fails. Make sure staff know how to use the equipment. If
  you're using online data collection tools, test them thoroughly before 
  starting data collection.
  
:::
::::

## Treatment: Traditional Methods [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296]

<br>

``` {r}
#| label: tbl-outdated

data.frame(Deletion = c("Listwise deletion", "Pairwise deletion", 
                        "Available Items Analysis", 
                        " "),
           Single = c("Mean Substitution", "Hot-Deck Imputation", 
                      "Last Observation Carried Forward", 
                      "Regression Imputation")) |> 
  kable(format = "html", 
        col.names = c("Data Deletion", "Single Imputation")) |> 
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, lightable_options = c("hover"), 
                html_font = "Lato") |> 
  column_spec(1:2, width = "4.5in")
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.3rem;"}

*Timing: ~ 66-68 min (1:06-1:08) by here.*

Despite prevention measures, you're still likely to have some missing data. 
There are two major types of traditional methods for handling missing data 
during analyses: data deletion and single imputation. 

Deletion-based methods are flawed because they inefficiently use the data.
Discarding data you've spent resources collecting is wasteful. Listwise deletion
drops every case that has missing data on any variable in the analysis. That's
the most common method because it is the default for many statistical packages. 
*It can only give you unbiased parameter estimates if the data are MCAR* and you 
still lose power due to shrinking sample size. But MCAR is a rare situation.
With MAR data, listwise deletion yields biased results and lower power. If there 
are high rates of missingness and patterns affecting many variables, you may not
have enough observations left to do meaningful analyses.

Pairwise deletion discards data at the variable level instead of the person
level. In a correlation matrix, pairwise deletion allows sample size to vary
depending on which variables you're correlating. That preserves some power, but
is still only unbiased under MCAR and it causes other problems, like difficulty
computing standard errors.

The available items method is used when combining multiple items to measure
constructs. If you've got a 6 item-scale for depression but only 4 items are
complete, you might take the mean of those 4 and use that as the scale score.
This makes strong assumptions about the interchangeability of items and about
the factor structure. It may decrease the variance between items and will often
decrease reliability. 

Single imputation methods replace each missing value with a single guess about
what value should be there. They fill holes in the original, incomplete
dataset to create a complete, imputed dataset. For example, one might swap in
the overall mean or a group mean for a variable, or use the hot deck method to
swap in the observed value from an randomly selected but otherwise similar case.
In longitudinal studies, sometimes people handle dropout by carrying the last
observed value forward to future time points. Meanwhile, regression imputation
uses complete variables to predict the values of incomplete variables, replacing 
missing values with the conditional mean of the missing variable given the 
observed predictors.

While single imputation methods increase power relative to deletion methods and
can work reasonably in some some narrow circumstances, they make untenable
assumptions, generally produce biased estimates even under MCAR and MAR
mechanisms, and underestimate standard errors. *Fortunately, there are better,*
*more modern methods available that don't suffer these drawbacks*.
:::
::::

## Treatment: Modern Methods [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296]

* Full-information maximum likelihood (FIML) estimation
* Multiple imputation (MI)

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 70-72 min (1:10-1:12) by here.*

I'm going to describe two modern, state-of-the-art methods for analyzing
incomplete datasets that solve the problems associated with the traditional
methods. Both full-information maximum likelihood estimation (FIML) and multiple
imputation (MI) can handle variables that are continuous, nominal, ordinal, or 
counts. Furthermore, they tend to produce the same results when applied well.  
:::
::::

## FIML (1) [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296]
FIML estimates parameters by combining observed data, relationships among 
observed variables, & distribution assumptions

:::{.incremental}
* Seeks parameter estimates that best fit the data
* Uses all available data without imputing any values
* Benefits from auxilliary variables 

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 71-73 min (1:11-1:13) by here.*

Let's take a closer look at the FIML method. It estimates parameters by 
combining observed data, relationships among observed variables, and assumptions 
about distributions. 

* ▶ It seeks the combination of parameter estimates that best fit the overall 
  dataset, which are the ones that make the observed data most likely given the 
  model assumptions. 
* ▶ The FIML computations take advantage of all the available data from each 
  case, whether it is complete or incomplete without imputing any of the missing 
  values. 
* ▶ FIML benefits from adding auxiliary variables to the model that either 
  predict missingness or are correlated with the incomplete variables. They 
  provide a way to recapture some of the information lost due to missing data. 
  Auxiliary variables are more useful when they're mostly complete and at least 
  modestly correlated with the incomplete variables, say around r =  0.40.
  
:::
::::

## FIML (2) [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296]

* Works well with MCAR & MAR data
* Yields biased estimates under MNAR

::::{.fragment}
:::{.callout-tip}
FIML is usually available in structural equation modeling (SEM) software. 
:::
::::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 72-74 min (1:12-1:14) by here.*

FIML is unbiased and yields accurate standard errors under both MCAR and MAR 
mechanisms, but of course can't fully solve the missing data problem under MNAR, 
though it does better than traditional methods. 

▶ If you want to use FIML methods, look in structural equation modeling software. 
Many familiar models--such as regression, ANOVA, and mixed models--can be 
translated into the SEM framework, which makes FIML reasonably accessible.
:::
::::

## MI (1) [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296; @van-Buuren-RN3962]

```{dot}
//| fig-width: 10
//| fig-height: 5

digraph MI {
graph [start=42, ordering=out];
rankdir="LR";

node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
OData [label = "Incomplete\nData"]

subgraph cluster_0 {
        label = "Create Imputed Datasets\n (m = 3)";
        fontname="Lato";
        style = "rounded, dashed"; 

        // Nodes inside the box
        node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
        
        IModel [label = "Imputation\nModel"]
        IData1 [label = "Dataset 1"]
        IData2 [label = "Dataset 2"]
        IData3 [label = "Dataset 3"]
        
        edge [fontname="Lato"]
        IModel -> IData1 [label = "Draw"]
        IModel -> IData2 [label = "Draw"]
        IModel -> IData3 [label = "Draw"]
        
        {rank = same; IData1; IData2; IData3}
    }

subgraph cluster_1 {
        label = "Apply Analysis Model to\nEach Dataset";
        fontname="Lato";
        style = "rounded, dashed"; 
        
        node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
        Result1 [label = "Model 1"]
        Result2 [label = "Model 2"]
        Result3 [label = "Model 3"]
        
        {rank = same; Result1; Result2; Result3}
    }

subgraph cluster_2 {
        label = "Apply Rubin's Rules";
        fontname="Lato";
        style = "rounded, dashed"; 
        
        node [shape = "box", style= "filled", fontname="Lato", fillcolor = "#C3FFEC"]
        Pooled [label = "Pooled\nModel\nParameters"]
    }
        
edge [fontname="Lato"]
OData -> IModel [label = "Fit"]
IData1 -> Result1 [label = "Fit"]
IData2 -> Result2 [label = "Fit"]
IData3 -> Result3 [label = "Fit"]
Result1 -> Pooled
Result2 -> Pooled
Result3 -> Pooled
}
```

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 73-75 min (1:13-1:15) by here.*

Multiple imputation uses all the available data but it works differently than
FIML because it uses two different models. This diagram is a high-level overview
of the multiple imputation solution to missing data. 

First you create an imputation model that uses relationships between variables
to predict the values of incomplete variables from observed variables. That
identifies the conditional distribution of each incomplete variable given the
observed variables. Drawing values from those distributions allows you to
replace each missing value. Doing that several times (using *m* to denote how
many) leaves you with a set of *m* complete, imputed datasets. 

Second, you apply the same analysis model to each imputed dataset. It must
reflect your hypotheses, using focal predictors and relevant covariates to
predict the outcome. Those analyses leave you with *m* sets of model parameters,
which will differ due to variability between the imputed datasets. Then, you use
Donald Rubin's rules for pooling the parameter estimates to get your final model
result.
:::
::::

## MI (2) [@Enders-RN8673; @McKnight-RN1296; @van-Buuren-RN3962]

:::{.incremental}
* Benefits from auxilliary variables
* Imputation model must contain analysis model
* Preserves uncertainty & quantifies influence of MD 
* Works well with MCAR & MAR data
* Can *sometimes* handle MNAR data 

:::

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 74-76 min (1:14-1:16) by here.*

▶ Like FIML, MI benefits from using auxiliary variables that either predict
missingness or are correlated with the incomplete variables. They go into the
*imputation* model instead of the *analysis* model. 

▶ Another key issue is that the imputation model must contain all effects that
will be in the analysis model to perform well.

▶ Pooling parameter estimates gives you final estimates that properly reflect the
uncertainty associated with replacing missing values with imputed ones. It also
quantifies the influence of missing data on parameter estimation, which is a
unique advantage of MI. No other method of handling missing data does that.

▶ MI is unbiased and yields accurate standard errors under both MCAR and MAR 
mechanisms, and 

▶ it can even handle MNAR data reasonably in some circumstances. 
:::
::::

## Where to Learn More

* Short overviews [@El-Masri-RN8683; @Fernández-García-RN4151; @Graham-RN1615; @Little-RN8684; @Newman-RN8223;  @Schafer-RN425] 
* Some good textbooks [@Enders-RN8673; @Graham-RN2677; @McKnight-RN1296; @van-Buuren-RN3962]
* Dealing with item-level missingness in scale scores [@Graham-RN1615; @Newman-RN8223]
* Advanced diagnostics [@Tierney-RN8958; @Weberpals-RN8957]
* Preventing missing data in: 
    * Clinical studies [@Wisniewski-RN2978]
    * Longitudinal studies [@Laurie-RN8257]
    * Surveys [@de-Leeuw-RN8682]

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 75-77 min (1:15-1:17) by here.*

This slide is just a way to point you toward specific, suggested resources where 
you can learn more about specific topics. The methods literature on handling 
missing data is extensive; this list is just to get you started. The full 
references are on a slide near the end of the presentation. 
:::
::::

## Software Tools

* SPSS: check out the `MVA` and `MULTIPLE IMPUTATION` syntax commands
* R packages: see the [CRAN Task View on Missing Data](https://cran.r-project.org/view=MissingData)
* Both FIML and MI are supported by Mplus and the R package lavaan 

::::{.notes}
:::{style="font-size: 1.5rem;"}
*Timing: ~ 76-78 min (1:16-1:18) by this slide.*

Several of major statistical software packages have tools for handling missing 
data. For example, SPSS has the `MVA` and `MULTIPLE IMPUTATION` commands.

If you use R, check out the CRAN Task View on Missing Data. It lists a large 
number of packages with specialized functions. 

SEM software like Mplus and the lavaan package for R support FIML estimation. 
:::
::::

## References {.scrollable}

::: {#refs}
:::

:::{.notes}
*Timing: ~ 77-79 min (1:17-1:19) by this slide.*

This slide contains all the references cited in the other slides. You can scroll
within this slide to see them all.
:::


# Discussion time! 
What questions do you have about missing data issues? 

::: {.notes}
*Timing: ~ 78-80 min (1:18-1:20) by this slide. Quit at ~ 90 min (1:30)*

So that was a short introduction to fundamental issues with missing data. We now 
have a little remaining time for questions and discussion. 
:::
